entry.title: Postal Payment with Third Party (Outgoing Payments) in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition
entry.content:
This blog post introduces a special payment method - postal money order in Slovakia and Czech Republic for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition.

When a payer wants to pay their payee through the post office, they first pay at the post office, and the payee can get the payment through this postal service.

In this blog post the focus is on the business scenarios with postal services in Czech Republic and Slovakia. When the payment got paid out to the payee. You generate a payment file for the outgoing payment using the payment format.

For the specific payment formats for the postal services:

  • Czech Republic - CZ_PSTLMONEYORDER_B
  • Slovakia -  SK_PSTLMONEYORDER_E

Prerequisite

Business Add-Ins
In the SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, based on your business requirements, you can implement the following business add-ins (BAdIs):

  • BADI_FKK_PAYM_CHRG_CALCULATE_C: For calculating the payment fee, for postal money orders.
  • BADI_FKK_PAYM_CHRG_POST_ENBL_C: To enable the posting of an additional document that includes both the base amount and the charge amount.
  • BADI_FKK_PAYM_CHRG_POST_PREP_C: For preparing the necessary information for posting a document that includes the base amount and the charge amount.

Note: To post additional documents with a total amount for payments and postal charges, you need to assign the business partner and the contract account. This master data is determined via External Identification in the BAdI implementation. Based on external identification - the Business Partner/Contract Account and assigned Bank details of Czech/Slovakian post offices are used in the documents

Bank Account Settings
Since the payments with money order are not directly paid out from the payer's bank account, instead it is paid out from the post office, it is necessary to create a fictive bank account and account ID for the post office.

Open the Manage Bank Accounts app.
For your company code, create a fictive Bank Account and Account ID for the Slovak post.


Define Payment Methods
Payments to payees through the post office essentially is processing payment. Therefore, you must select a payment method and assign it to the payment medium format.

  1. Open the Define Payment Methods configuration activity (ID: 101952) in the SAP Fiori Launchpad.
  2. Select the pre-delivered payment formats or use your own configured formats.
    Sample values:
    Payment MethodPayment Mediums Format
    P - Money order ESK_PSTLMONEYORDER_E
    P - Money order ECZ_PSTLMONEYORDER_B
  3. Assign Third-Party Postal Payment as the payment medium format.

Note to Payee
You can maintain the Note to payee for the payment medium in the Maintain Note to Payee Type for Payment Medium (ID: 103949) configuration.

Define Parameters for Paying Out

For Postal Money Order B, you maintain sender identification, for example a six-digit numeric value that uniquely identifies the sender. The number is assigned to the sender by the post when the agreement is settled.

Fill in the Customer number in the Define Parameters for Foreign Payment Transactions (ID: 101953) configuration.


Procedure of Generating Payment with Postal Money Orders

  1. Set up the master data of business partner with payee's address only (payee's bank information isn't required). The Slovak Post finds the payees with their address to pay out.
    Set up also the master data for the post office as the following example:
  2. Post a payable item that you need to pay to the payee.
  3. Execute a payment run and clear the payable documents. The total amount of the contract accounting document is posted. You can see the information in the Log details as follows.
  4. In the next phase, the following actions are required for the two entities involved.
    • For your house bank: Run a payment run and generate a payment file for the bank. Your bank then transfers money to the account of the post office.
      Note: This condition serves as a prerequisite for the post office to print out the pay slips for the payee.
    • For the post office: Generate a payment medium file with the necessary information that is sent to the post office. If the money arrives in the post office's bank account, the post office prints out the pay slip and pay to the payee.

Is this blog post helpful to you? Leave comments below to let us know your thoughts.

entry.title: Demo Scenario: Clean Core Extensibility with ABAP Cloud and SAP Build
entry.content:
Co-Authors: @Sivakumar_Subramaniam , @ShivaniShankar 

A common feedback when talking about clean core extensibility is the absence of demo-ready content to visualize these guidelines that have evolved over time. This blog post attempts to take an extensibility scenario as example and showcase the equivalent scenario in a clean-core compliant world. Nevertheless, we cannot claim to be covering every possible flow in one post. The intent is to showcase one example covering a wide range. In the process, we walk through some of the decisions taken that go beyond technology and consider business aspects as well. A lot of technical details are omitted since this post is already quite long. Look forward to the discussions and feedback in the comments section to assess interest and demand for similar posts/more technical versions. 

Recommended Pre-read: 

While an effort is made to abstract technical complexity to a great extent, a reader still needs some basic understanding of the SAP ecosystem, how extensions work, why they exist and some background of clean core.

To maximize takeaways from this blog post, consider reading the clean core extensibility whitepaper before you start, particularly if "Clean Core" sounds unfamiliar. Due to the rich context, answers to why clean core etc available in the whitepaper already, we skip it here. Nevertheless, a lot of what this blog post covers would not make much sense without this background/context. 

Key Assumptions: 

1. We consider level-A of clean core extensibility since this is the highest standard. If one were to target level-B for example, the target architecture might look a little different with potential reuse of certain objects

2. For simplicity and to keep the focus on extensibility, we assume there is no fit-to-standard solution available for this scenario which may or may not be factually correct. In a real project, please consider fit-to-standard analysis and an analysis of your existing custom-developed artefacts before developing something new

Scenario Description : 
- Provide a capability to add custom gift cards when creating sales orders in SAP Cloud ERP Private (S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition)

- On applying the gift card, a discount is added to the sales order by an amount equal to the gift card amount

- On creation of a sales order, loyalty points need to get accrued against the membership of the business partner (from the sales order). For simplicity, assume only business partners of type "Customer"

- The loyalty program has tiers and based on the tier the customer is on, he/she/they accrue a percentage of the sales order value as points

- It shall be possible for a customer to request an upgrade to their loyalty program tier. In this case, approval from an admin is required before the change is active 

Scenario Refinement:  

Understanding/context: When working with sales orders, this company has 2 parallel reward programs. One, a gift card that can be applied on creation of the sales order for an immediate discount. Another, a loyalty program where one accrues points with each sales order. Potentially, for redemption (not in scope here) through different means at a later point in time

Tasks:

- Since gift cards need to be applied, there is also an unsaid need to be able to create and maintain these gift cards (by an admin). This means an app to create/maintain gift cards with appropriate fields

- To be able to apply the gift card when creating a sales order, an option is needed directly in the sales order application (or transaction)

- The applied gift card should impact the value of the sales order before creation (not post-processing) 

- Similarly, loyalty program tiers would need to be maintained (possibly as business configuration)

- The loyalty points are calculated based on the created sales order and don't have a dependency or need to update/modify the consistency of the sales order itself. This could be handled as a separate activity from the creation of the sales order

Solution Architecture (Classic Extensibility - non-Clean Core level-A):In the classic extensibility scenario which is non-clean core level-A, we use the following technology choices to build this - 

  1. Gift Card Maintenance - WebDynPro Application
  2. Capability to add gift card to sales order in VA01 (Transaction code for sales order creation) - User Exit
  3. Loyalty Management Application - Module Pool Program
  4. Tier upgrade - Classic workflow

 Solution Architecture (with ABAP Cloud and SAP Build - Clean Core)

In the clean-core level-A compliant extensibility scenario, we use the following technology choices to build this - 

  1. Gift Card Maintenance - Application developed using RAP (ABAP RESTful Application Programming Model) with on-stack developer extensibility. Yes! with real code written by a developer inside the S/4HANA system outside of key user tools and still the highest level of clean core! If this is against your understanding of clean core, read SAP's official whitepaper (section 3.2 at the time this blog post is written) linked in the pre-read section of this blog post!  
  2. Capability to add gift card to sales order in the standard sales order Fiori app  - Key User Extensibility
  3. Loyalty Management Application - Side-by-side extension using BTP ABAP Environment
  4. Maintenance of tiers of the loyalty program - Business Configuration in the BTP ABAP Environment (not shown in the demo except as value help)
  5. Tier upgrade - SAP Build Process Automation
  6. Communication between S/4HANA and BTP - Event Mesh (or Advanced Event Mesh)

Alternate options - The Loyalty Points Application could also have been developed side-by-side using CAP (Cloud Application Programming Model) with additional handling for tier maintenance. 

Decision Criteria (Technical and Business): 

  1. The use case around application of gift card has a very tight dependency on the sales order. The standard SAP sales order application needs to understand and bring up the gift cards for a value help, have the gift cards applied consistently across impacted business objects when the sales order is updated. All of this needs to happen as part of the sales order creation/update process in S/4HANA. This makes on-stack developer extensibility and key-user extensibility good candidates. 
  2. The loyalty points application has 2 factors to consider - Side-by-side v/s on-stack and CAP v/s ABAP Cloud
    • Side-by-Side v/s On-Stack:
      • Decision: Side-by-Side
      • In private cloud/on-premise, SAP ships a new major version every 2 years. By moving out of the core (S/4HANA) anything that can be moved logically without adverse performance/over-engineering, etc, the customer can benefit from always being on the latest release and getting the latest updates. This de-risks innovation from upgrade project timelines and upgrade strategy. 
      • Enforcement of clean core - With the side-by-side approach, clean core is enforced with syntax checks etc since ABAP Cloud is the only option on BTP ABAP Environment as opposed to governance based approaches on-stack which may need an ABAP Cloud package and processes to ensure a developer doesn't slip back to the familiar classic extensibility when cloud ready alternatives are available.
    • CAP v/s ABAP Cloud
      • Decision: Both options are valid. Here, ABAP Cloud due to developer skill set and business configuration handling know-how. Since each option has strengths and benefits, refer the BTP Developer's guide for details. To generalize any of the two as always the better option without case-to-case analysis and comparison may be an unfair/sub-optimal decision. 

General question on BTP ABAP Environment cost -
"The cost of BTP ABAP Environment is higher" - Apart from the smaller runtime size now available, which reduces costs, the cost needs to be spread out across applications being developed. One instance of the BTP ABAP Environment can be reused for multiple applications so long as the runtime/memory requirements are within available limits. Compare this to additional costs for CF Runtime (for example) for each deployed app with CAP and the costing at solution level at scale tends to be similar. This is not to say CAP is a bad option, just that when the decision is influenced by cost, how this cost is calculated decides quite a bit! When this becomes equivalent, the models can be compared on best fit for the use case on technical/architectural grounds.

Demo of functional scenario in both approaches along with ATC findings across levels - B, C, D on the package where classic extensibility was used

The following demo showcases the scenario with classic extensibility as well as with Clean Core Level-A. It also touches upon ATC checks on the package containing the classic extension. To understand how ATC (ABAP Test Cockpit) helps categorize the findings, refer section 3.3.4(at the time of writing) of the clean core extensibility whitepaper (in the pre-read section)

 

Call to Action: Comment below what you would wish was better covered in the post and what key takeaways you had! Your inputs help us understand and validate where such blog posts can add more value

entry.title: Unable to Assign Collection Management Attributes via API in S/4HANA Public Cloud Integration
entry.content:
Hello SAP Experts,

We are currently working on an integration between our S/4HANA Public Cloud system and a Microsoft Dynamics 365 system for customer creation and updates.

As part of this integration, we are using the API_BUSINESS_PARTNER inbound service along with its related entities such as A_BusinessPartner, A_BusinessPartnerAddress, A_BusinessPartnerContact, A_BPRelationship, A_BPContactToAddress etc

During the process, we are creating the following roles for each customer:

  • FLCU00 (Customer – Financial Accounting)
  • FLCU01 (Customer – Sales Area)
  • UDM000 (Collections Management role)

While the roles are successfully created, we are facing challenges with Collections Management data. Specifically, we are unable to assign the following attributes to the customer via the integration:

  • Collection Segment
  • Collection Group
  • Collection Strategy
  • Collection Specialist

We have not been able to identify any API or entity within the Business Partner service that supports maintaining these collections-related attributes.

During our analysis, we found several SAP-delivered jobs, such as:

  • Assignment of Collection Profiles to Business Partners
  • Changing Segment Data
  • Replacing Collection Specialists

However, these jobs do not fully meet our requirements. For example, the Assignment of Collection Profiles to Business Partners job requires a fixed “Created On” date parameter. This makes it difficult to automate daily processing, as we cannot dynamically define a rolling date range (e.g., “today” or “today - 1”) when scheduling the job.

As a result, we are struggling to design an automated solution that ensures Collections Management attributes are consistently assigned to newly created customers.

We would greatly appreciate any guidance on:

  • Available APIs or extensibility options to maintain Collections Management data
  • Recommended best practices for automating this process in S/4HANA Public Cloud
  • Any alternative approaches that others have successfully implemented

Our goal is to ensure that our Accounts Receivable team can effectively use the Process Receivables app with complete and accurate customer data, including assigned collection profiles and contacts.

SAP Integration Suite SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition SAP S/4HANA business partner #

entry.title: Re: SAP Public cloud - Sales BOM for Service item (TAD)
entry.content:
Service item categories (SERV) are not designed to be part of a sales BOM explosion. The item category determination logic in Public Cloud is simplified and locked down and you cannot freely assign SERV to explode under LUMF.

Some workarounds are

1) Model services differently
Instead of trying to explode a service item in a sales BOM, model the service as a separate sales item (manual entry or template). You can use product bundles or solution orders (if available in your scope) to combine materials and services in one order.

2) Use configurable product bundles (SAP Best Practice)
Public Cloud supports bundling logic where you can sell a package of goods + services. This is the recommended way to handle “service with material” scenarios, rather than forcing BOM explosion.

3) Alternative: Service BOM in Project/Service Management
If the requirement is to plan services together with materials, consider using Project Billing / Service Orders instead of Sales BOM. These processes allow you to combine service and material items in one structure.

entry.title: Posting of Periodic Accrual Amounts
entry.content:
To post accrual amounts at the end of a period, you can use the transaction ACEPOSTINGRUN in the S/4HANA Accrual Engine.

  • This transaction is used to post periodic accruals for all applications that work with the Accrual Engine.
  • On the selection screen, you’ll find the field “Application Component”:
    • If you want to post manual accruals, enter ACAC in this field.
  • The fields Accrual Object and Accrual Subobject depend on the application component:
    • For manual accruals, the Accrual Object is a combination of the accrual object category and the accrual object number.
    • Use the F4 input help to select the correct value for this field.

 

Posting of Accruals: Define Posting Schema

The posting schema is used to define how accrual postings are structured.

The Posting Schema Specifies:

  • How many line items are to be created in an accrual posting
  • Which symbolic G/L accounts are to be used in each line item
  • What line item type is to be assigned to each line

Important Line Item Types:

  • A line item of type 9003 must be included for periodic accrual postings
  • A line item of type 9000 may be posted to any G/L account, typically an expense account

Standard and Custom Schemas:

  • Standard posting schemas such as SAP_ACE_PP are delivered by SAP
  • Custom posting schemas can be created by customers in the IMG activity "Define Posting Schema.

 

 

Posting of Accruals: Assign Posting Schema

  • For each accrual item type that triggers accrual postings, a posting schema must be assigned.
  • This assignment is carried out in the IMG activity:
    Assign Accrual Item Types to Journal Entry Types and Posting Schemas

Posting Schema Assignment Rules:

  • If an accrual item type is configured to perform all types of postings, it must be assigned to three posting schemas, one for each posting type (ACE TType):
    • IP – Opening Posting
    • PP – Periodic Posting
    • FP – Final Posting
  • If only periodic postings are required, the accrual item type must be assigned to one posting schema, for ACE TType = PP.

 

Additional Requirements:

  • Each assigned posting schema must include a line item with type 9003 (required for periodic accruals).
  • In the same IMG activity, the document type to be used in accrual postings is also assigned.

entry.title: Re: How to handle subcontracting procurement between grou...
entry.content:
Options to design this in Public Cloud

1) Subcontracting PO (your idea)

Company A issues a subcontracting PO to Company B for the repair service. B consumes its own raw materials and labor internally. Costs are invoiced back to A via the PO.

Advantage: Simple, standard, supported in Public Cloud.
Limitation: You won’t see the detailed raw material consumption in A’s books — only the repair service cost.

2) Intercompany Service PO

Instead of subcontracting, treat the repair as a service procurement. A issues a PO for a “Repair Service” material/service master. B delivers the service and invoices A. Raw material and labor costs are absorbed in B’s cost accounting, then passed to A via the service price.

Advantage: Clean intercompany process, aligns with Public Cloud best practice.
Limitation: Less visibility into the BOM/raw material detail at A.

3) Intercompany STO + Service Billing

If you want to track the physical flow of goods (returned product moving from A to B), you can use a Stock Transport Order (STO) to send the product to B. B repairs it, consumes raw materials, and then returns the product to A. The repair service is billed separately via an intercompany service invoice.

Advantage: Full visibility of product movement + service cost.
Limitation: More complex (two flows: STO for goods, service PO for repair).

So the recommended Best Practice is

  • Use intercompany service procurement (PO for repair service).
  • If physical product movement must be tracked, combine it with an STO.
  • Subcontracting PO is technically possible, but it is designed for scenarios where the buyer provides components. Since A does not provide raw materials, service procurement is cleaner.

entry.title: Re: Best practices for restricting physical deletion of S...
entry.content:
Dear @Harukiy,

The clean-core way to prevent physical deletion of sales order items is to use the released Cloud Badi SD_SLS_CHECK_DELETE_ITEM. SAP confirms that item deletion is technically prevented by setting the NO_DELETION flag the message is only used to inform the user. SAP also recommends using Reason for Rejection when the sales order should no longer be processed instead of deleting the document

More details below:

https://me.sap.com/notes/0003152093

The logic should block deletion only when Reason for Rejection is still empty on the sales order item. The relevant item field is:

salesdocumentitem-salesdocumentrjcnreason

Badi implementation example:

IF salesdocument-salesdocumenttype = 'TA'  AND

   salesdocumentitem-salesdocumentrjcnreason IS INITIAL.

  no_deletion = 'X'.

  APPEND VALUE #(

    messagetype = 'I'

    messagetext = |Deletion for item | &

                  |{ salesdocumentitem-salesdocumentitem } | &

                  |not allowed. Please use Reason for Rejection first.|

  ) TO messages.

ENDIF.

The implementation should be created in the Custom Logic app because Public Edition supports released Badi through key-user custom logic

More details below:

https://learning.sap.com/courses/implementing-sap-s-4hana-cloud-public-edition/using-key-user-in-app-extensibility-to-create-custom-business-logic

Summary:

This solution blocks physical deletion only when the item has no Reason for Rejection. Once the user maintains SalesDocumentRjcnReason the deletion check no longer blocks the action

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: HFL sub asset creation
entry.content:
Dear experts, 
Hope you are doing well!

I would like to ask for your support on one SAP topic related to the subsequent capitalization of parts/kits on HFL equipment… We are currently discussing the correct SAP treatment for capitalization of parts/Kits used in our project…

The main open point is how this can be handled technically in SAP, especially because the kit itself is not a standalone serialized equipment item.
The situation is as follows:

  • We have a main equipment item / hardware with a serial number, which can be defined as equipment in SAP.
  • In addition to that, we have an Kit, which is a bundle of materials such as pipes, hoses, and similar components.
  • The kit has a part number, but it is not serialized and would normally not be defined as separate equipment or as a standalone asset.
  • However, the intention would be to capitalize these costs and depreciate them together with the main equipment.

Could you please help confirm what is technically possible in SAP and what would be the correct process / instruction? I need instructions how to process it in detail from SAP technical side. what  type of transaction to use and how to use it. From my current understanding the sub-asset creation would be the preferable method.


My process flow how I think it might be done:

Thank you very much!!

entry.title: Re: Deleting records from replicated table
entry.content: Hi, yes: remote table, real-time replication. The issue is that the source table is subjected to a truncate every morning and then repopulated with "in scope" data for that day, which is then updated every ten minutes. This is designed to keep the volume down on the source table. I then need to replicate said source table into Datasphere, which I am doing via real-time replication. Triggers don't work on truncate (and I cannot switch the source to delete due to logging and performance). I cannot use upserts as the source tables don't have identifiers that show last update date and reloading the whole table is prohibitive.

entry.title: Provisioning of Business Data Cloud : SAP Hana Cloud
entry.content:
As a continuation of the series on the Introductory Guide to Provisioning SAP Business Data Cloud and its various components, this blog will help you provision SAP HANA Cloud within Business Data Cloud. Please refer to the different parts to get a holistic understanding of the entire process.

Part 1: Provisioning of Business Data Cloud through SAP4Me
Part 2: Provisioning of SAP Datasphere
Part 3: Provisioning of SAP Analytics Cloud
Part 4: Provisioning of SAP Databricks
Part 5: Installation of Intelligent Apps and Data Packages
Part 6: Provisioning of SAP HANA Cloud

#Part 6
In this blog, we will discuss in detail how to provision SAP HANA Cloud via Business Data Cloud Core.

Prerequisites

  • An active SAP Business Data Cloud subscription with a valid Entitlement System ID.
  • Access to SAP for Me (me.sap.com) with the appropriate provisioning roles.
  • SAP Business Data Cloud Core must be enabled — you will see the confirmation banner on the Applications tab.
  • A configured SAP Cloud Identity Services (CIS) tenant is available in your landscape.
  • Sufficient remaining Capacity Units in your contract to allocate to the new solution.
Note: The provisioning wizard is a guided, multi-step flow. Each step must be completed in sequence. You can navigate back using the Previous Step button to revise entries before finalising
 
Provisioning SAP HANA Cloud creates a single SAP HANA database instance within an SAP-managed application tenant. When the HANA database instance is provisioned, the SAP HANA Cloud tools are automatically set up as well. These tools, accessible from the SAP HANA Cloud tenant in SAP for Me, allow you to:

  • Manage the database instance (see Managing SAP HANA Cloud Instances), including resizing, adding or removing replicas, and creating ECNs.

  • Work with the database using SAP HANA Database Explorer (see Getting Started with SAP HANA Database Explorer).

  • Monitor the instance’s capacity unit consumption (see Consumption Monitor in SAP HANA Cloud Central).


Step-by-Step Provisioning Guide

Log in to SAP for Me nd navigate to Portfolio & Products > Business Data Cloud. On the Applications tab you will find all BDC applications that are available for provisioning within your entitlement, including:

  • SAP Analytics Cloud
  • SAP Databricks
  • SAP Datasphere
  • SAP Datasphere, Data Composer
  • SAP HANA Cloud
  • SAP Business Data Cloud Connect
  1. A green confirmation banner confirms that SAP Business Data Cloud Core is enabled, and provisioning can begin. Click Start Provisioning on the SAP HANA Cloud tile

     

  2. In the "Provision SAP HANA Cloud" dialog that appears, provide details such as Solution Name, Path and Business Type.  The Business Type available by default is 'Production’


  3. On the Assign Quota page, specify the number of Capacity Units to allocate to the SAP HANA Cloud instance within this solution. 

     

     For now, you can use the HANA Cloud estimator from SAP Discovery Centre. 
    https://hcsizingestimator.cfapps.eu10.hana.ondemand.com/sap/hana/estimator/index.html 
    The BDC Calculator does not yet include SAP HANA Cloud. When estimating HANA Cloud capacity within BDC, please run the following interim approach: run the HANA Cloud estimator and multiply the result by 0.836 to calculate the equivalent BDC Capacity Units. This short-term workaround will be replaced soon.

  4. The Set Up Systems step configures the underlying system integrations required for your HANA Cloud solution. Two integrations must be addressed:

    • Integration with SAP HANA Cloud — It is set to default as add a new system to let the wizard provision a fresh HANA Cloud system on your behalf. This is the recommended approach for new deployments.
    • Integration with SAP Cloud Identity Services — Choose the CIS tenant that will handle identity and authentication for this solution (e.g. the CIS tenant that is configured with your BTP Cockpit).

       

  5. Input the details in the Configure Parameters. Define the instance-level parameters for SAP HANA Cloud. These settings control the infrastructure characteristics of the HANA database that will be provisioned. Select the Region, give an instance name and select the preset options.
    See also Data Center Availability in SAP Business Data Cloud.

  6. Formations define how BDC integrates the provisioned systems into a cohesive, connected landscape. On this page, configure the formation that links your HANA Cloud instance to the BDC solution. The Formation Type is pre-populated based on the selected BDC application. The Formation Name is a unique identifier within your landscape — choose a descriptive name that reflects the solution's purpose or environment.

  7. The final Review page presents a consolidated summary of all configuration choices made across the previous five steps. Verify each section carefully:

    • 1. General Information — Entitlement System, Solution Name, Path, Business Type.
    • 2. Assign Quota — Capacity Units allocated to SAP HANA Cloud.
    • 3. Set Up Systems — SAP HANA Cloud system and CIS tenant selection.
    • 4. Configure Parameters — Region, Instance Name, Preset.
    • 5. Set Up Formations — Formation Type and Formation Name.

    Each section includes an Edit link for inline corrections without losing the rest of the configuration. Once satisfied, click Finish to submit the provisioning request. The HANA Cloud instance will begin provisioning in the background.


  8. A confirmation message notifies you that the system is now creating the solution. Choose View in Resources to view the tenant in the resource group you have indicated


  9. While the solution is being created, the status in the Resources tab is Processing, and it will reach Ready status when provisioning is complete.



  10. Once the tenant is ready, you will be able to access the tenant from the link under the ‘Resource’ tab and under the tenant details
  11. Under ‘Customer Landscape’, you can see the system details of the new tenant. Also, the system integration is visually presented, making it easier to understand and navigate through your system landscape
  12. As a next step, you will have to add the tenant to the ‘Formation’ that you have created already and add your BDC tenant to it
  13. Navigate to me.sap.com > Products & portfolio > Business Data Cloud > Customer Landscape > Formation
  14. Choose the correct Formation and then click on ‘Include System’ and choose the Databricks tenant to include in the formation, and save the changes
  15. You can add one or more HANA Cloud systems in a single formation. You can also add the same system in multiple formations

Next Steps:

The SAP HANA Cloud instance is provisioned with a DBADMIN user with a generated password.

  • Log in to the IAS (Identity Authentication Service) tenant you selected for integration with the instance. Assign the “HANA Cloud Security Administrator” role to a user. This user will then be able to configure the DBADMIN account using SAP HANA Cloud tools. For additional details, refer to Managing SAP HANA Database Instances Using SAP HANA Cloud Central.

 Log in to the HANA Cloud Central and configure the DBADMIN user

  • As a next step, log in to the SAP HANA Database Explorer 


Additional Information:

After integrating an SAP Business Data Cloud system with SAP HANA Cloud, you need to share the required data products with the SAP HANA Cloud system through the catalogue (see Sharing Data Products to SAP Systems).

In SAP HANA Cloud, you must then perform additional steps to install the shared data products. For guidance, refer to Consuming Data Products in SAP HANA Cloud in the SAP HANA Cloud documentation.

Now, you have successfully provisioned SAP HANA Cloud within the BDC Core.

Happy Learning

Regards,
Shreyasri Bose

entry.title: Re: Provisioning of Business Data Cloud : SAP Hana Cloud
entry.content:
Good detailed technical walkthrough  especially useful for anyone working with SAP Business Data Cloud + HANA Cloud provisioning.

What stands out here is how structured the provisioning flow is becoming (SAP for Me → quotas → systems → formations → review). It really shows how SAP is moving toward a more guided, cloud-native setup experience, rather than manual configuration-heavy deployment like older HANA environments.

The inclusion of Capacity Units + estimator adjustments is also interesting  it highlights how resource planning is now tightly integrated into deployment decisions, not something handled after the fact.

Overall, this is a solid reference for understanding how SAP is aligning Datasphere, Databricks, and HANA Cloud under one unified data landscape.

entry.title: Mastering Product Carbon Footprints: New Learning Journey for SAP Sustainability Footprint Managemen
entry.content:
In today’s business landscape, moving beyond high-level corporate reporting is no longer optional. To drive real decarbonization, we must understand the emissions embedded in every single product—from raw material extraction to the factory gate.

With SAP Sustainability Footprint Management (SFM), companies can automate these complex calculations and gain precise insights into the environmental impact of their entire product portfolio.

Why Product Carbon Footprint (PCF)?

While a Corporate Carbon Footprint (CCF) provides an overview of an organization’s total emissions, the PCF breaks this data down to the product level. This granularity allows businesses to:

  • Identify emission hotspots within the supply chain or specific manufacturing steps.

  • Make data-driven decisions during product design and material sourcing.

  • Ensure transparency for customers, partners, and regulatory bodies.

What You Will Learn in This Learning Journey

This specialized course builds on the fundamentals of carbon accounting and dives deep into the world of product-related emission calculations. The focus is on integrating business data and applying rigorous calculation models.

Key Highlights of the Training:

  • Bridging CCF and PCF: Understand the relationship between corporate and product footprints and how to allocate emissions across your product lines.

  • Master Data Management: Learn to integrate relevant data from ERP systems, define organizational boundaries, and set up calculation variants.

  • Energy Flow Modeling: Use energy flow models to assign Scope 1 and Scope 2 emissions directly to the resources used in the manufacturing process.

  • Cradle-to-Gate Calculation: Capture emissions across the entire lifecycle—from raw material purchase and transport to the finished product.

  • Scalability at Core: Discover how SAP SFM handles millions of products and complex Bills of Materials (BOMs) to provide automated, audit-ready results.

Who Is This Course For?

This Learning Journey is designed for:

  • Sustainability Leads and Consultants looking to implement PCF strategies.

  • Data Analysts tasked with connecting sustainability metrics with operational ERP data.

  • Product Managers aiming to optimize the environmental footprint of their product range.

Start Your Learning Journey Today!

Are you ready to take the next step toward a transparent and sustainable value chain?

👉 Access the Learning Journey: Establishing the Product Carbon Footprint with SAP Sustainability Footprint Management

Join the SAP Learning Group to connect with fellow learners and get your questions answered by experts. Let’s make sustainability measurable together!

Did you find this overview helpful? Have you started working with SAP Sustainability Footprint Management yet? Share your thoughts and experiences in the comments below!

entry.title: Re: Partner-2-partner collaboration in the construction industry
entry.content:
This is a strong example of how SAP’s ecosystem is shifting toward partner-driven innovation in the construction industry.

What stands out most is the focus on connecting BIM directly with ERP systems like SAP S/4HANA. That bridge between design data and real execution (budgeting, supply chain, procurement) is exactly where a lot of construction inefficiencies usually happen.

The build.works approach is interesting because it’s not just a tool  it’s positioning BIM as an operational system, not just a design system. That’s a big shift for prefab builders, contractors, and suppliers since it enables real-time coordination across the entire project lifecycle.

Also, the clean core + partner solutions strategy SAP is pushing here is clearly designed to scale industry-specific innovation without overloading the core ERP system.

Overall, this kind of collaboration shows how construction tech is moving toward fully integrated, cloud-based project ecosystems rather than siloed tools.

entry.content: The error is almost always because the outbound delivery has not been PGI’d yet. In STO, GR is only possible after PGI at the supplying plant. Once PGI is posted, the receiving plant can perform GR without issue.

entry.title: WT Creation Issue in S/4HANA Public Cloud EWM – Foreground vs Background Behavior
entry.content:
Guys, I am facing an issue related to WT creation in S/4HANA Public Cloud EWM.

What I did was: I created a custom storage type, defined a Putaway Control Indicator (PCI), and maintained the Storage Type Search Sequence (STSS). I also assigned the STSS to the storage type and mapped the PCI with the STSS using the configuration node “Specify STSS in SIMG.”

Then, I assigned the PCI to the product master and posted a GR. When I tried to create a WT for putaway, the system created the WT with a standard bin for putaway (S970 – clarification zone bins).

However, when I created the WT using the “Change Inbound Delivery – Deliveries” app, the system created the WT for my custom bin.

In simple terms, foreground WT creation (via Fiori app) is working as expected, but background WT creation (via Warehouse Monitor) is not.

The same issue occurs for outbound as well. I created an activity area and assigned it to the storage type, defined a stock removal indicator and assigned it to the product master, and also maintained the storage type search sequence with the removal indicator.

This setup works in the “Manage Outbound Delivery Orders” app, but not in the Warehouse Monitor, where it shows the bin determination error mentioned below.

I hope I made my problem statement clear. Waiting for your responses

entry.title: Customizing Accrual Item Types for Utilization Postings
entry.content:
Utilization posting in the accrual engine refers to the process of consuming accruals when actual costs are posted, such as through invoice postings. The system reduces the accrual amount by the actual cost amount specified in the invoice. 

Accrual Item Creation: 

  • For each calculated amount, the Accrual Engine creates a separate accrual item in the accrual object, with the accrual item type being the key field. 
  • Therefore, each calculated amount requires a unique accrual item type. 

Accrual Item Types: 

  • Accrual amounts require an accrual item type, such as: 
  • ACCRL_STD for Standing Accruals 
  • ACCRL_PER for Periodic Accruals 
  • ACCRL_PMA for Plan Minus Actual approach 
  • Actual costs require an accrual item type (e.g., ACTCST) when utilization postings are performed. 
  • Planned costs require an accrual item type (e.g., PLNCST) only when using the Plan Minus Actual approach. 

Linking Accrual Item Types: 

  • To enable the Accrual Engine to reduce accruals when actual costs are posted, define relationships between accrual item types in the IMG activity 'Define Accrual Item Types': 
  • Link accrual item types for accruals, actual costs, and (for Plan Minus Actual approach) planned costs. 
  • An actual cost item type is required for all ACCRL* item types to achieve utilization postings. For Plan Minus Actual approach, this item type is also needed to calculate accruals. 

Plan Minus Actual Approach: 

  • The planned costs item type is only required when using the Plan Minus Actual approach, specifically for item type ACCRL_PMA. 
  • To ensure proper functionality, configure these settings and relationships in the system as described above. 

Assigning Accrual Item Types: 

Assign accrual item types that perform postings (ACCRL*) to transaction types and posting schemas. 

ACTCST and PLNCST are used solely for calculation and are not assigned to any posting schema. 

  • Configure these assignments in the IMG activity 'Assign Accrual Item Types to Journal Entry Types and Posting Schemas.' 

Posting Schema Assignments: 

  • For utilization and release postings, assign all ACCRL* item types to the following posting schemas: 
  • UP (utilization posting) 
  • RP (manual release posting) 
  • FP (automatic release posting) 
  • Additionally, assign specific posting schemas based on the accrual item type: 
  • ACCRL_STD to IP (opening posting) 
  • ACCRL_PER and ACCRL_PMA to PP (periodic posting) 

 

Creating Accrual Objects for Accrual and Utilization Postings: 

When creating an accrual object (e.g., using transaction ACACTREE02) for accrual and utilization postings, enter the following accrual items with the required accrual item types: 

Accrual Item for Accruals: 

  • Accrual Item Type: ACCRL* (* stands for _STD, _PER, or _PMN, depending on the scenario) 

Accrual Item for Actual Costs: 

  • Accrual Item Type: ACT_CST 

Accrual Item for Planned Costs ( Plan Minus Actual approach only): 

  • Accrual Item Type: PLN_CST 

Postings and Displaying Amounts: 

  • After creating the accrual object, perform postings with reference to it (e.g., supplier invoices or manual journal entries using transactions FB01, FB50, etc.). 
  • Once postings are made, the accrual item will display these amounts (e.g., in transaction ACACTREE02). 

Creating Accrual Objects for Utilization of Standing Accruals: 

For the Utilization of Standing Accruals scenario, enter two accrual items when creating the accrual object: 

Accrual Item for Accrual Amount: 

  • Accrual Item Type: ACCRL_STD (negative total amount) 
  • Posting Schemas: IP (opening posting) and UP (utilization posting) 

Accrual Item for Actual Costs Calculation: 

  • Accrual Item Type: ACTCST 
  • Accrual Method: ACT_COSTS 
  • Ensure both accrual items have the same transaction currency 

Customization: 

  • Assign accrual item types to journal entry types and posting schemas in the Customizing activity 'Assign Accrual Item Types to Journal Entry Types and Posting Schemas.' 

Standing Accruals: 

  • No accrual method is needed for ACCRL_STD as no periodic accruals are required. 

Utilization of Periodic Accruals: 

  • When creating the accrual object, enter two accrual items: 

Accrual Amounts: 

  • Accrual Item Type: ACCRL_PER 
  • Performs periodic and utilization postings (PP and UP transaction types) 
  • Assigned to a posting schema in the Customizing activity 'Assign Accrual Item Types to Journal Entry Types and Posting Schemas' 

Actual Costs: 

  • Accrual Item Type: ACTCST 
  • Accrual Method: ACT_COSTS 
  • Must have the same transaction currency as the other accrual item 

Plan Minus Actual Approach: 

  • When creating the accrual object, enter three accrual items: 

Accrual Amounts: 

  • Accrual Item Type: ACCRL_PMA 
  • Performs periodic and utilization postings (PP and UP transaction types) 
  • Accrual Method: PLN-ACT ("Plan minus actual") 
  • Assigned to a posting schema in the Customizing activity 'Assign Accrual Item Types to Journal Entry Types and Posting Schemas' 

Planned Costs: 

  • Accrual Item Type: PLNCST 
  • Accrual Method: Any (e.g., LINEAR) 

Actual Costs: 

  • Accrual Item Type: ACTCST 
  • Accrual Method: ACT_COSTS 

  • All three accrual items must have the same transaction currency

entry.title: Re: Displaying issues with Eclipse Modeling Tools
entry.content:
Hello @jacques_renaux2 ,

Try the below options ones,

Change Eclipse theme → Switch to Light theme (Window → Preferences → Appearance).

Disable HiDPI scaling → Add -Dswt.autoScale=false in eclipse.ini.

Update / downgrade tools → Install latest BW Modeling Tools patch or revert to previous Eclipse (4.38/4.36).

Reset workspace → Start Eclipse with a new workspace.

 

Thanks,
Sravan

entry.title: Plateau Report Designer: Sementtime is one hour earlier than the class time in the system
entry.content:
Hello,

I'm having an issue where, after adjusting the class roster, the time displayed in the SementInfo_Child section is always one hour earlier than what's entered in the class. The time zones in the class, agenda, user, instructor, and admin sections are all set correctly. This isn't a problem in the original report. I made the following adjustments in that section: All instructors are retrieved, not just the primary one, and the institution description is also displayed.

Does anyone know why the incorrect time is being retrieved? No adjustments were actually made to that part.

Here’s the query:

select rownum as seg_num,ssg_seg_num, seg_desc,
seg_startdate, seg_starttime,
seg_enddate, seg_endtime,
instructor_lname, instructor_fname, instructor_mi,
fullname,
loc_desc,TimeZone, displayTimeZone,general_display_name, jp_desc, facility_desc
from (select ss.ssg_seg_num, ss.seg_desc seg_desc,
ss.start_dte seg_startdate, ss.start_tme seg_starttime,
ss.end_dte seg_enddate, ss.end_tme seg_endtime,
nvl(i.lname,'') instructor_lname, nvl(i.fname,'') instructor_fname, nvl(i.mi,'') instructor_mi,
concat(concat(concat(concat(COALESCE(i.lname,''),', '),COALESCE(i.fname,'')),' '),COALESCE(i.mi,'')) as fullname,
l.locn_desc loc_desc, s.timezone_id TimeZone, s.display_in_schd_tz displayTimeZone, stud.general_display_name, jp.jp_desc, pf.facility_desc
from pa_sch_seg ss, pa_locn l, pa_ssg_locn sl, pa_ssg_inst si, pa_sched s, pa_inst I,pa_student stud, pa_job_pos jp, pa_facility pf
where ss.schd_id = ? and
ss.schd_id = s.schd_id
and ss.schd_id = sl.schd_id (+)
and ss.ssg_seg_num = sl.ssg_seg_num (+)
and sl.locn_id = l.locn_id (+)
and sl.primary (+) = 'Y'
and ss.schd_id = si.schd_id (+)
and ss.ssg_seg_num = si.ssg_seg_num (+)
and si.inst_id = i.inst_id (+)
and i.stud_id=stud.stud_id (+)
and stud.dmn_id = jp.dmn_id (+)
and s.facility_id = pf.facility_id (+)
order by ss.start_dte, ss.start_tme)

 

Thank you!

 

#PRD #Plateau #LMS PRD #Plateau Report Designer

Best regards,
Jessy

entry.title: IAS: loginName whitespace handling (lack of normalization / UI validation feedback) is intentional?
entry.content:
Hi community,

I would like to share a technical observation regarding SAP Identity Authentication Service (IAS) that may have implications for identity consistency in authentication and provisioning scenarios. Probably most people dealing with IAS already know, but i  cannot find any information or clarification/hint on this. And from a data/normalization/sanitization perspective, i find this really weird.

And since loginName has special relevance in SSO/IDP setups compared to other IAS keys, i wanted to share this obsevation - especially since the mail field in IAS usermanagement gives feedback for whitespaces as invalid input.

Observation

The loginName attribute in IAS appears to preserve leading and trailing whitespace characters without normalization or sanitization, e.g.:

  • "username"
  • "username " (trailing space)
  • " username" (leading space)
  • "   username   " (also both and multiple)

These values are treated as distinct identities at the data layer.

Expected behavior (identity systems perspective)

From a general identity management and authentication design standpoint, one would typically expect one of the following behaviors:

1. Input normalization

  • Automatic trimming of leading/trailing whitespace for loginName
  • Consistent canonicalization before persistence and/or lookup

2. Strict validation with feedback

If whitespace is considered semantically invalid:

  • Reject input at UI level
  • Provide explicit validation message (similar to email format validation, where it throws error for leading and trailing whitespaces being invalid for input)

Actual behavior observed

  • Whitespace is accepted and persisted in loginName
  • No visible UI indication that whitespace is part of the stored value: so the value appears to have no trailing/leading whitespaces, while it may have multiple
  • No validation or warning is triggered during input or selection
  • The UI representation appears visually identical to a trimmed value, despite backend difference

Impact on identity flows

This behavior can introduce subtle inconsistencies in:

  • SAML / OIDC identity mapping
  • ADFS-based federation scenarios
  • Downstream applications relying on strict string matching
  • User provisioning / identity correlation logic

In practice, this may result in:

  • Silent authentication mismatches
  • Hard-to-diagnose SSO failures for individual users
  • Inconsistent identity resolution across systems

Technical concern

The core concern is the lack of canonicalization of identity identifiers at ingestion or persistence level, combined with:

  • Absence of UI-level feedback for non-visible characters (whitespace)
  • No validation layer enforcing normalization rules for loginName

This creates a discrepancy between:

  • what the UI displays
  • what is stored
  • what is evaluated during authentication/matching

Question to the community / product team

  • Is whitespace preservation in loginName intentional by design?
  • Is there any roadmap consideration for:
    • input sanitization
    • configurable normalization rules
    • UI validation for non-visible characters?

Note

I initially considered raising this via SAP Support, but the incident classification flow appears strongly oriented toward operational tenant/admin scenarios, and does not clearly expose a category for identity-attribute normalization or data consistency behavior. Hence I am posting this here for clarification and discussion.

If this info is redundant/old please don't judge me. I would have reported this as a "bug" or "issue", but i cannot find the right spot - and i am also a friend of sharing insights.

  • Make this field available in field catalog, condition tables, and access sequence.
  • Maintain pricing via VK12 to enable Transportation Zone–based automatic pricing.

entry.title: Postal Payment with Third Party (Outgoing Payments) - SAP S/4HANA incl. Cloud Private Edition
entry.content:
This blog post introduces a special payment method - postal money order in Slovakia and Czech Republic for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition, and SAP S/4HANA.

When a payer wants to pay their payee through the post office, they first pay at the post office, and the payee can get the payment through this postal service.

In this blog post the focus is the business scenarios with postal services in Czech Republic and Slovakia. When the payment got paid out to the payee. You generate a payment file for the outgoing payment using the payment format.

For the specific payment formats for the postal services:

  • Czech Republic - CZ_PSTLMONEYORDER_B
  • Slovakia -  SK_PSTLMONEYORDER_E

Prerequisite

Business Add-Ins

Based on your business requirements, you can implement the following business add-ins (BAdI), and activate the function modules:

  • Activate the function module FKKID_EVENT_0671 in event 0671.
    Then you can implement the BAdI (Business Add-In) BADI_FKK_PAYM_AMOUNT_ROUND_C for the payment rounding.
  • Activate the function module FKKID_EVENT_0630 in the event 630.
    Then you can implement the BADI (Business Add-In) BADI_FKK_PAYM_CHRG_CALCULATE_C to calculate the additional fees for postal services.
  • Activate the function module FKKID_EVENT_1799 in the event 1799. Then you can implement the BADI (Business Add-In) BADI_FKK_PAYM_CHRG_POST_ENBL_C and BADI_FKK_PAYM_CHRG_POST_PREP_C to post additional document with a total amount for payments and postal charges for bank transfers.
    Note: To post additional documents with a total amount for payments and postal charges, you need to assign the business partner and the contract account. This master data is determined via External Identification in the BAdI implementation. Based on external identification - the Business Partner/Contract Account and assigned Bank details of Czech/Slovakia post offices are used in the documents

Bank Account Settings

Since the payments with money order are not directly paid out from the payer's bank account, instead it is paid out from the post office, it is necessary to create a fictive bank account and account ID for the post office.

Open the Manage Bank Accounts app.
For your company code, create a fictive Bank Account and Account ID for the Slovak post.

Define Payment Methods

Payments to payees through the post office essentially is processing payment. Therefore, you must select a payment method and assign it to the payment medium format.

  1. Open the Define Payment Methods activity using the view cluster VC_TFK042Z in the transaction SM34.
  2. Select the pre-delivered payment formats or use your own configured formats.
    Payment MethodPayment Medium Formats
    P - Money order ESK_PSTLMONEYORDER_E - Postal Payment Order, Slovakia
    P - Money order ECZ_PSTLMONEYORDER_B - Postal Payment Order, Czechia
  3. Assign Postal Remittance for Payment, Slovakia (SK_PSTLMONEYORDER_E) as the payment medium format.

Note to Payee

You can maintain the Note to payee for the payment medium in the Maintain Note to Payee Type for Payment Medium activity using the view cluster VC_TFK042V in the transaction SM34.

Define Parameters for Paying Out

For Postal Money Order B, you maintain sender identification, for example a six-digit numeric value that uniquely identifies the sender. The number is assigned to the sender by the post when the agreement is settled.

Fill in the Customer number in the Define Parameters for Foreign Payment Transactions activity using the view TFK012D in the transaction SM30.

Procedure of Generating Payment with Postal Money Orders

  1. Set up the master data of business partner with payee's address only (payee's bank information isn't required). The Slovak Post finds the payees with their address to pay out.
    Set up also the master data for the post office as the following example:

  2. Post a payable item that you need to pay to the payee.
  3. Execute a payment run and clear the payable documents. The total amount of the contract accounting document is posted. You can see the information in the Log details as follows:
  4. In the next phase, the following actions are required for the two entities involved:
    • For your house bank: Run a payment run and generate a payment file for the bank. Your bank then transfers money to the account of the post office.
      Note: This condition serves as a prerequisite for the post office to print out the pay slips for the payee.
    • For the post office: Generate a payment medium file with the necessary information that is sent to the post office. If the money arrives in the post office's bank account, the post office prints out the pay slip and pay to the payee.

Is this blog post helpful to you? Leave comments below to let us know your thoughts.

entry.title: Latest People Profile MDF object UI
entry.content:
First of all. Is it possible to configure custom UI for the MDF objects?

The answer is YES. However, it is no longer used the Configure People Profile to assign the created UI. 

Now, this UI configuration is set directly on the object itself. On the "Default Screen" option.

Yes! you must set an  UI (designed at manage configuration UI) as the default screen in Configure Object Definition page. This configuration will impact on the LPP Card. 

 

Does the UI Rules still work on the Latest PP?

Yes, for example the "hide fields" rules still work.

 

Can I show an MDF object twice?

No, Each MDF object can be added only once.

Hope this helps!

entry.title: [S/4HANA Private Cloud] Flexible Workflow – Sales Order Remains Blocked After Auto-Skip
entry.content:
I’m facing an issue with Flexible Workflow for Sales Orders.

Requirement

  • ZCS + condition ZP06 : trigger approval
  • ZCS without ZP06 : auto-release (no approval)

Implementation

  • BAdI SD_APM_SET_APPROVAL_REASON : sets approval reason Z005 for all ZCS
  • Workflow created in Manage Workflows for Sales Orders
  • Custom condition evaluator ZSWF_WORKFLOW_CONDITION_EVA

Important Constraint

  • BAdI runs before COMMIT WORK
  • Pricing data (ZP06 in PRCD_ELEMENTS) not yet persisted
  • SELECT not allowed due to transactional contract FUNCTIONAL

 Therefore, launch condition based on ZP06 is unreliable

Workaround Applied

  • Launch condition = NONE
  • Step condition = check ZP06 (via custom evaluator after COMMIT)

Behavior

  • With ZP06 : approval works 
  • Without ZP06:
    • Step condition = FALSE → step skipped 
    • Workflow log = Completed (SWI1) 
    • BUT order remains blocked 

Error

V2468 – Sales document is being processed for approval

Observation

  • No pending work items
  • Workflow WS02000006 = Completed
  • Status in VA03 = In Approval Process

Root Cause (suspected)

If step is skipped:

  • releaseSalesOrder action is never triggered
  • Approval status is not reset
  • Document remains locked

Question

How can we handle this standard gap?

  • Is there a way to force release when no approval step is executed?
  • Any BAdI / enhancement to reset approval status?
  • Or recommended design pattern for this scenario?

entry.title: Re: [S/4HANA Cloud] Even after deleting the parent item's...
entry.content:
Dear @shiwa,

Yes, if you expect the existing planned orders for the component materials to be deleted/recalculated the component materials also need to be included in the MRP run scope. They do not necessarily have to be entered manually one by one but the MRP run must cover them for example through plant, MRP controller, product group or material scope. Running MRP only for the parent material will not automatically clean up planned orders that belong to component materials outside the selected run scope. SAP confirms that Planning Mode 3 Delete and Recreate only affects materials included in the MRP run. If the material is not included or if there is no planning-relevant change and the material is skipped, existing MRP elements may remain. SAP recommends using Planning Mode 3 together with Regenerative Planning when full reprocessing is required

More details below:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3608136

SAP also explains that in MRP Live the planning scope can be defined broadly, for example for all materials/plants or by product groups, MRP controller, or material scopes. During MRP  the BOM is exploded and dependent requirements for components are determined as part of the planning run

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/2bba750d1e124e1ea2a039bb1cd9b6c5/d2abce5314894208e10000000a174cb4.html

https://learning.sap.com/courses/implementing-sap-s-4hana-cloud-public-edition-manufacturing/exploring-the-concept-of-mrp-and-getting-introduced-to-mrp-live_f71d99ce-e5b9-4dc4-acda-da56f64008ef

Summary:

Do not rely on an MRP run for the parent item only. For component planned orders to be deleted/recreated the component materials must be part of the MRP run scope

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Integrating Business Partner from a S/4HANA Cloud Public to another S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition
entry.content:
Introduction

In integrating SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition systems, Communication Management plays a critical role in securely exchanging data. This blog explains how to configure communication between two SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition systems using standard SAP communication scenarios. The blog is co-written with @Karunaharan. 

 

Prerequisites

  • Access to the source/sender SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition system.
  • User must have the role, ADMINISTRATOR.
  • Target SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition system details, such as Host Name, Logical System, and Business System.

 

Step 1: Create A Communication User

Use the “Maintain Communication Users” app to create a technical communication user. Make sure to provide a username and password, as both are required. Ensure that the same user is also created in the target system. This user will be used for sending and receiving data. 

1. Open the Maintain Communication Users app.

2. Choose New.

3. Enter a valid username and description.

4. Either provide a password (basic authentication) or upload certificates for certificate-based authentication.

5. Choose Create to save the new user.

OAuth or Client Certificate can be used if required. 

 

Step 2: Create A Communication System

To enable communication between two systems, you must register these systems in the Communication Systems app (that is, register the target system in the source and vice versa.

For inbound communication, your registered system calls the services provided by SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition. For outbound communication, your registered system provides the services called by SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition.

1. Open the Communication Systems app.

2. Choose New

3. Provide an ID and a name for your system.

4. On the General tab, under the Technical Data section, enter the Host Name, Logical System, and Business System details of the target system.

5. In the Users for Inbound Communication tab, choose Add (+)

a. In the New Inbound Communication User dialog box, enter the username and authentication method.
(created in Step 1: Create A Communication User)

b. Click on Ok Button.

 6. In the Users for Outbound Communication tab, choose Add (+)

a. In the New Outbound Communication User dialog box, for the Authentication Method field, choose Username and Password (the same user must be added as an inbound user in the target system).

b. Click on the Create Button.

 7. Click on the Save Button.

 

Step 3: Create A Communication Arrangement

1. Open the Communication Arrangements app.

2. Choose New.

  1. Select the scenario SAP_COM_0008.
  2. Provide a name for the communication arrangement.
  3. Choose Create.

3. In the Common Data section, select the communication system
(created in Step 2: Create A Communication System)

4. Activate SOAP services using the “Service Status” checkbox.

5. Maintain Replication Model, Replication Mode, and Output Mode. More information about these properties is available subsequently. 

6. Click on the Save Button.

7. Use the “Check Connection” button to verify connectivity between systems

 

More Properties in the Outbound SOAP Service

Replication Modes

Initial Replication: Replicates all existing data from the source to the target system.

Change Replication: Only the changes made in the source system will be replicated to the target system.

 

Output Modes

Direct: Replication happens immediately after saving the changes made to the Business Partner object..

Pooled: Replication occurs periodically. In this case, only the Business Partner objects are replicated without unchanged relationships.

 

System Filter

System Filter controls the data transferred between systems. It allows only certain data to be copied or synchronized based on specific conditions.

For example:

  • Transfer only certain types of data (like sales orders or customer data).
  • Limit the data based on specific attributes (like a region or department).

 

Additional Properties in Communication Arrangement

Data Controller and Purpose

Defines the responsibility and purpose of data usage to ensure compliance with data protection regulations. The combination of data controller and purpose must be the same in both source and target systems.

 

Integration with C4C Enabled

Enables integration-specific services required for SAP C4C integration.

 

SOAP Compatibility Mode

Ensures compatibility with SOAP-based services and avoids message processing errors.

 

Conclusion

Following these steps ensures a secure and reliable integration between two SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition systems using standard communication scenarios.

entry.title: Re: SPPC Revenue Recognition Key for Enterprise Projects
entry.content:
Good day @MananDurge 

Thank you for your detailed explanation and screenshots.

Based on your scenario, I would not recommend that you use the SPPC Periodic Services revenue recognition key for an Enterprise Project as a workaround.

Note that KBA 3656776 only explains how you can manually enter a revenue recognitions key that is not available in the dropdown because it is technically marked as incompatible. It does not mean that every manually entered key will work correctly in the revenue recognition process. Manual entry option is only allowed under specific prerequisites, such as no EBRR postings or cleared EBRR postings.

In your case, the error during the revenue recognition run there an example of one that is not compatible. The system is expecting revenue plan data that belong to the Customer Project or Professional Services process logic, but the object is an Enterprise Project. Therefore, the key can be typed manually, but the follow-on revenue recognition calculation cannot complete because the required revenue planning basis is not available in the same way.

For Enterprise Projects, the revenue recognition key is relevant where a project with revenue is assigned to one or more sales order items. See KBA 3412809 on the relevance of recognition key and its importance for company code, item category, and material combination of the sales order item.

So, the answer to your question is: no, there is no recommended workaround to use SPPC as a Customer Project periodic services key for an Enterprise Project if the revenue recognition run cannot find the required revenue plan data.

The better approach is one of the following:

  • Use as option 1 the correct Enterprise Project revenue recognition key. The key should be derived from the sales order item through the relevant configuration combination. 
     
  • Option 2 is for you to use Customer Projects if the business process is Periodic Services. SPPC belongs to that process logic, so using it avoids the mismatch between the project type and the revenue recognition method. 
  • Option 3 is to review your sales order item setup and its derivation. See KBA 3427750 for more insights.

Also note that KBA 3394591 gives details on the general rules required for you to change a revenue recognition key.

If you also see errors like “Update of Revenue recognition key to master data failed” or “An exception was raised”, is suggest you check whether a project draft exists. See KBA 3263368 for more details.

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Re: cloudalm move events to new configuration
entry.content: After migrating to RISE, Cloud ALM assigns a new technical system ID, and event monitoring doesn’t automatically carry over. To continue Integration, Exception, and Job Monitoring, you need to reconfigure them under the new system ID by recreating the monitoring setup manually, since variants aren’t migrated. In practice, this means documenting your old setup and then re‑establishing the same monitoring definitions against the new technical system in Cloud ALM.

entry.title: What backup options are available in SAP Commerce Cloud and how to restore them?
entry.content:
SAP Commerce Cloud comes with built-in backup capabilities, but the documentation around automated backups and how to actually use them in practice is not always easy to find. This post aims to collect the most common questions in one place.

We are looking for clear answers on the following:

  1. How often are backups created automatically in SAP Commerce Cloud? What types of backups exist and what is the difference between them? What role does Microsoft Azure play in the backup process, and is there any way to interact with Azure-level backups directly?

  2. Where can existing backups be found? Are all backup types visible in the Cloud Portal, or are some of them only accessible through SAP support?

  3. What is the process to restore a backup in case of an emergency? How long does a restore typically take, and does the answer differ depending on whether it is a database-only restore or a full restore including media?

Any information on retention periods, self-service vs. SAP-assisted restore, and limitations (e.g. media not included in certain backup types) would also be very helpful.


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: What backup options are available in SAP Commerce Clo...
entry.content:
There are actually two separate backup mechanisms running in SAP Commerce Cloud, and understanding the difference between them is key to answering all three questions.

1. How often are backups created and what is Azure's role?

SAP Commerce Cloud uses Microsoft Azure SQL as its database engine, which includes automated backups that are enabled by default and run continuously in the background with no manual action required:

  • Full database backups: every week
  • Differential backups: every 12 to 24 hours
  • Transaction log backups: approximately every 10 minutes

This means the maximum potential data loss in an automated restore scenario is around 10 minutes. These backups are retained for 35 days across all CCv2 environments.

Customers do not have direct access to these backups through the Azure Portal or any self-service interface. They are managed entirely at the infrastructure level by SAP and Azure.

In addition to the automated Azure backups, it is also possible to create backups manually via the Cloud Portal. There are two types available:

Standard Data Backup covers both the database and media files. It does not expire until manually deleted. Depending on the size of the database and the number of media files, it can take up to several hours to complete.

Quick Data Backup covers the database only, leveraging Azure Point-in-Time Restore technology. It can be created in minutes and automatically expires after 35 days.

2. Where can backups be found?

Automated Azure backups are not visible in the Cloud Portal or Azure Portal on the customer side. They exist at the infrastructure level and are not directly browsable or accessible.

Manually created Standard and Quick Data Backups are fully visible in the Cloud Portal under the backup section of each environment and can be managed there directly as a self-service feature.

Backup limits per environment type:

  • Standard Data Backups: maximum 3 in production, maximum 2 in non-production
  • Quick Data Backups: maximum 4 in any environment type

3. How and how fast can backups be restored in an emergency?

The process depends on which backup type you are working with.

For automated Azure point-in-time restore (database only): this is not a self-service feature. A support ticket must be raised with the following information: the database name, the exact point in time to restore to within the 35-day retention window, the reason for the restore, a risk acknowledgement, and approval from the customer side. Response time depends on the ticket priority. For a Very High priority ticket, initial response is within one hour in most cases, and may be less depending on the support agreement in place.

Important: point-in-time restore covers the database only and cannot be used to restore media. If media files were also changed and need to be reverted, use a Cloud Portal Standard Data Backup restore instead to ensure consistency between the database and media.

For Standard Data Backup restore (database and media): this is self-service via the Cloud Portal. Restore time depends on the size of the database and the volume of media files and can range from minutes to several hours. After any restore, a new deployment in recreate mode is typically required to bring the environment back online.

For Quick Data Backup restore (database only): also self-service via Cloud Portal and faster than a standard restore since it uses point-in-time restore technology.

A few additional points worth knowing:

  • A maintenance page is recommended during restore to prevent data from being modified while the operation is in progress
  • Indexes are included in the restore, so no re-indexing is required after completion
  • If a restore is cancelled midway, services will remain down until the next deployment is triggered

Further reading:

For more details on all of the above, the following resources cover these topics in depth:

KBA 3308351 - FAQ for Data Backup and Restore - SAP Commerce Cloud
https://me.sap.com/notes/3308351/E

KBA 3395752 - Point-in-time database restore on Commerce Cloud
https://me.sap.com/notes/3395752/E

SAP Community blog - Quick Data Backup for SAP Commerce Cloud
https://community.sap.com/t5/crm-and-cx-blog-posts-by-sap/quick-data-backup-for-sap-commerce-cloud/ba-p/14325384
